data.title: Top economist predicts sustained shift into small-cap, value stocks driven by resilient economy and Fed policy. Rotation away from large growth stocks as small-caps gain momentum. During earnings season, large-cap growth stocks have declined 10-12% relative to value. Small-caps could lead market advance without requiring significant earnings growth. Fed pivot and economic resilience support broader market leadership. (Word count: 76)
data.description: Investors are focusing on market shifts rather than headline inflation, as highlighted by Professor Jeremy Siegel. He notes a sustainable move towards small-cap and value stocks, backed by economic strength and Federal Reserve policy. Professor Siegel points out a shift away from large growth names towards healthier market progress, with large-cap stocks retracting by about 10% to 12% compared to value stocks. This shift is seen as more enduring than past reversals driven by temporary volatility. Small-cap stocks are gaining momentum, with Siegel suggesting they do not need significant earnings growth to thrive; stable earnings alongside Fed actions could lead to broader market leadership. Despite inflation concerns, resilience in the market remains strong. For more details, visit Benzinga.com.

data.title: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent criticizes the Federal Reserve for losing $100 billion yearly due to 'mistimed asset purchases,' calls for greater accountability.
data.description: U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has voiced his disapproval of the Federal Reserve, citing various ethical failings and policy errors that he believes have led to losses of several billion dollars annually. In a critique on Tuesday, Bessent highlighted that the Federal Reserve was facing a $100 billion yearly loss due to what he called "mistimed asset purchases." Specifically, he pointed to bond acquisitions made during the pandemic, which were followed by a significant rise in interest rates. Speaking to CNBC from Davos, he expressed regret over these decisions, labeling them as a major blunder. Bessent also raised concerns about other ethical lapses within the central bank in recent times. The detailed story can be found on Benzinga.com.
